intent: Carry Scheme B forward unchanged and refresh only its exact pre-G1 decomposition dossier and trace relations.

entity.title: Primitive graph for rejected-score disclosure and appeal selection
entity.summary: A benchmark-bound decomposition in which only the rejected applicant's information partition changes and the common final review mapping is the score-blind, strictly review-monotone class phi(s,r)=psi(r), with phi(s,r)=r as its deterministic baseline. This version re-anchors that unchanged Scheme-B specification in the refreshed pre-G1 decomposition package.

nodes.label: Binary qualification state
nodes.economic_meaning: Nature draws a full-support qualification state shared by both disclosure regimes; it is the latent object forecast by the institutional, applicant, and review signals.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Both qualification states have strictly positive prior probability.
The prior is identical under decision_only and score_disclosure.

nodes.label: Ordered institutional score
nodes.economic_meaning: The institution observes an informative score with low, middle, and high values ordered by qualification posterior; low and middle are distinct positive-probability rejected categories.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Low, middle, and high score probabilities and their qualification ordering are fixed across regimes.
Low and middle both occur with positive probability among rejected applicants.

nodes.label: Fixed initial acceptance rule
nodes.economic_meaning: The institution accepts a high score and rejects low and middle scores before any appeal; this rule creates the pooled rejection event in the benchmark.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The same initial rule applies in both regimes.
Initial participation and application behavior are held fixed.

nodes.label: Private post-rejection signal
nodes.economic_meaning: After rejection the applicant privately observes a strictly informative binary signal about qualification before deciding whether to appeal.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Both signal realizations occur with positive probability in every relevant rejected-score cell.
The signal technology is identical across disclosure regimes.

nodes.label: Fresh review signal
nodes.economic_meaning: An appeal produces a fresh binary review signal r, coded r=1 for favorable and r=0 for unfavorable evidence.  Write rho_q=Pr(r=1|qualification=q), with 0<rho_0<rho_1<1 in the maintained informative full-support baseline.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The review technology and the favorable/unfavorable coding are fixed across regimes.
The maintained baseline has 0 < rho_0 < rho_1 < 1, and the review signal is realized only after the appeal choice.

nodes.label: Score-blind strictly review-monotone final mapping
nodes.economic_meaning: The common final rule is score-blind and depends only on the fresh review signal: phi(s,r)=psi(r), where 0<=psi(0)<psi(1)<=1.  The minimal deterministic benchmark sets psi(0)=0 and psi(1)=1, hence phi(s,r)=r: final acceptance occurs exactly after a favorable fresh review.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Score blindness requires phi(low,r)=phi(middle,r)=psi(r) for each review realization r.
Strict review monotonicity requires psi(1)>psi(0); the same fixed psi applies under decision_only and score_disclosure.
The hand-solvable baseline is deterministic, psi(0)=0 and psi(1)=1, and the rule never infers the private applicant signal from the appeal act.

nodes.label: Applicant value and appeal cost
nodes.economic_meaning: A rejected applicant receives a common positive value from final acceptance and pays a common positive cost to appeal.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Value and cost are identical across regimes and applicant cells.
Indifference is resolved in favor of appeal.

nodes.label: Conditional-independence normalization
nodes.economic_meaning: Institutional score, applicant signal, and review signal are conditionally independent given qualification as an explicit parsimonious baseline rather than a claimed economic force.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: This normalization is common to both regimes.
Any relaxation that lets the disclosed score forecast review noise beyond qualification is a robustness extension, not part of the benchmark delta.

nodes.label: Rejected-score information refinement
nodes.economic_meaning: This is the sole treatment difference: decision_only reveals only that the score is low or middle, whereas score_disclosure reveals the realized low or middle score before the same private signal and appeal choice.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: All priors, signal laws, payoffs, initial and final rules, and review technology remain fixed.
No no-appeal, mandatory-appeal, pricing, full-score, altered-review, capacity, or rationing regime is a coequal benchmark.

nodes.label: Regime-contingent Bayesian posterior
nodes.economic_meaning: Let p be the applicant's posterior qualification probability after the regime-specific rejection message and private signal. Under conditional independence and the score-blind rule, the anticipated acceptance probability is A_psi(p)=psi(0)+[psi(1)-psi(0)]*[p*rho_1+(1-p)*rho_0].  In the minimal phi(s,r)=r benchmark this reduces to p*rho_1+(1-p)*rho_0.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Applicants know the maintained prior, signal technologies, rho_0, rho_1, and the common rule psi and update Bayesianly.
Because rho_1>rho_0 and psi(1)>psi(0), A_psi(p) is strictly increasing in p; disclosure changes it only through the refined score message.

nodes.label: Posterior-threshold appeal choice
nodes.economic_meaning: With acceptance value v>0, appeal cost c>0, and the no-appeal continuation payoff normalized to zero, a rejected applicant appeals exactly when v*A_psi(p)-c>=0.  Indifference is resolved in favor of appeal.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Non-appellants remain finally rejected, so their continuation payoff is zero under the maintained normalization.
Disclosure changes the appeal set only when the disclosed-cell and pooled-rejection values of v*A_psi(p)-c lie on different sides of zero.

nodes.label: Application, disclosure, appeal, and review timing
nodes.economic_meaning: Nature draws qualification and signals; the institution applies the initial score rule; the rejected applicant receives the regime-specific message and private signal; the applicant chooses whether to appeal; an appeal then generates fresh review information and the fixed final rule is applied.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Disclosure is truthful, committed, and costless within the model.
The fresh review signal is realized only after the appeal choice.

nodes.label: Positive per-appeal processing burden
nodes.economic_meaning: Each appeal consumes a fixed positive amount of institutional processing resources; the frame includes no hard capacity, rationing, congestion, or endogenous review quality.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: The per-appeal burden is constant across regimes.
Total processing burden changes only through the mass of appeals.

nodes.label: Classification and processing outcome vector
nodes.economic_meaning: The institutional comparison separately records total appeal load, qualified appellants, corrected qualified rejections, review-induced acceptances of unqualified applicants, appellant composition, and total processing burden without imposing welfare weights or a sign theorem.
primitive_sufficient_conditions: Every delta is oriented score_disclosure minus decision_only.
A strict frontier interpretation requires an additional capacity or optimization mechanism and is not supplied by a positive per-appeal burden alone.

edges.economic_meaning: Qualification changes the likelihood of each institutional score.
Qualification changes the likelihood of the applicant's private signal.
Qualification changes the likelihood of the fresh review signal.
The fixed initial rule maps the observed score into acceptance or the pooled set of rejected categories.
Only rejected low and middle scores enter the information-partition comparison.
Pooling versus revealing the rejected score changes the information used in Bayesian updating.
The private post-rejection signal combines with the disclosed message in the applicant's posterior.
The baseline factorization disciplines how score and private-signal likelihoods combine.
The favorable review indicator r enters the score-blind mapping phi(s,r)=psi(r); the minimal benchmark applies phi=r.
The fixed pair psi(0), psi(1) converts the posterior-implied review distribution into A_psi(p), which enters the appeal payoff.
The posterior p determines A_psi(p), and the applicant appeals if and only if v*A_psi(p)-c>=0.
Acceptance value and appeal cost determine the common appeal threshold.
Selection into appeal determines volume, composition, and which cases can be corrected or erroneously accepted.
The fixed final mapping determines the classification consequences conditional on the selected appeal pool.
A fixed per-appeal burden maps appeal volume into total processing load without creating a capacity frontier.
The applicant chooses after the disclosure message and private signal but before fresh review information.

entity.title: G1 dossier for rejected-score disclosure and appeal selection
entity.summary: An unconfirmed refreshed G1 dossier that records Scheme B as resolved, preserves the exact benchmark, and retains the separate frontier-terminology and fresh-audit requirements.

requirements.description: The question identifies the rejected-score disclosure perturbation, the appeal-selection object, and the separate classification and processing outcomes without imposing a directional or welfare claim.
The benchmark set contains exactly decision_only, and score_disclosure changes only the rejected applicant's information partition while every other maintained primitive is fixed.

The potential importance is a transparent separation of appeal volume, appellant composition, corrected qualified rejections, erroneous final acceptances, and processing burden; this is a bounded contribution radius rather than a claim about transparency, welfare, or policy generally.

The researcher selected the score-blind class phi(s,r)=psi(r), psi(1)>psi(0), with phi(s,r)=r as the deterministic baseline; PrimitiveGraph version 3 states its implied acceptance probability and appeal payoff.

Park or kill the framing if no admissible nonempty open region changes a positive-probability appeal cell, or if all results remain mechanical threshold enumeration with no interpretable posterior ordering, selection decomposition, or sufficient statistic.

A positive per-appeal burden supports an outcome-vector comparison but does not by itself define a strict feasible-set or optimization frontier; capacity, rationing, congestion, endogenous review, or welfare aggregation remain outside scope unless a later human framing decision adds them.
No human G1 decision is recorded in this dossier; a human must confirm the core question, exact benchmark scope, and any final-review primitive before they become stable dependencies.

payload.rationale: Scheme B resolves the final-review mapping gap while preserving the exact decision_only benchmark and all no-sign, no-welfare, and no-capacity boundaries.  G1 remains unconfirmed and this dossier still recommends revision because the ResearchQuestion's strict frontier terminology remains a separate named repair and the refreshed frame must pass a new economics audit.

outcome.rationale: The refreshed package preserves Scheme B, marks only the final-rule requirement resolved, retains the separate RQ terminology repair, and does not confirm G1.
